title: Tax-loss harvesting: a practical guide

image.alt: Stock chart data displayed on a monitor in a modern office

body: Tax-loss harvesting is the practice of selling an investment at a loss to offset capital gains elsewhere in your portfolio. The result is a lower tax bill in the current year. Done well, it can save thousands of dollars annually without changing your overall investment exposure.

Here is how it works. Suppose you hold Fund A, which is down $20,000 from your purchase price. You also hold Fund B, which you plan to sell for a $30,000 gain. If you sell Fund A first, you realise a $20,000 loss. That loss offsets $20,000 of the gain from Fund B. You pay capital gains tax on $10,000 instead of $30,000.
After selling Fund A, you reinvest the proceeds into a similar (but not identical) fund that tracks the same market segment. Your portfolio allocation stays the same. Your tax bill goes down.

Timing matters. Losses are most valuable when they offset short-term gains, which are taxed at ordinary income rates. Long-term gains are taxed at lower rates, so the benefit of offsetting them is smaller. If your losses exceed your gains in a given year, you can deduct up to $3,000 against ordinary income and carry the rest forward to future years.

The wash-sale rule is the main constraint. The IRS disallows a loss if you buy a "substantially identical" security within 30 days before or after the sale. This means you cannot sell an S&P 500 index fund at a loss and immediately buy the same fund back. You can, however, buy a different S&P 500 fund from another provider, or a total market fund with similar but not identical holdings.

Some advisors harvest losses only in December. That approach misses opportunities. Markets fluctuate throughout the year. A fund might be down 12% in March and flat by December. Harvesting in March captures a loss that would otherwise disappear.

We run tax-loss harvesting continuously. Our system flags positions with unrealised losses above a minimum threshold every trading day. When a position qualifies, we evaluate whether selling and replacing it improves the client's after-tax return without creating unintended tracking error.

Who benefits most? Clients in high tax brackets with large taxable portfolios. If your marginal federal rate is 37% and you harvest $50,000 in short-term losses, the tax savings exceed $18,000 in that year alone. The replaced position continues to grow. You have deferred the tax, not eliminated it, but the time value of that deferral compounds over decades.

Tax-loss harvesting is not free. Transaction costs, bid-ask spreads, and the complexity of tracking cost basis all factor in. For small accounts or low-turnover portfolios, the benefit may not justify the effort. For portfolios above $500,000 in taxable accounts, it is one of the most reliable ways to improve after-tax returns.
The key is consistency. One-time harvesting in a down year helps. Systematic harvesting across every market condition, integrated with your broader tax strategy, helps more.
